title: Quick check: OAuth, endpoints, and webhooks

questions.prompt: Your provisioning script obtains an access token at 09:00 and starts a long batch of Cloud PBX creates that runs until 11:30. What's the right token-handling approach?
choices: Use the same access token throughout; it stays valid as long as the connection is active.
Refresh the access token using the refresh_token grant before the 30-minute expiry, capturing the new access_token and refresh_token from each refresh response.
Re-run the client_credentials grant every time the token expires, since refresh tokens are not supported.
Cache the access token in plaintext on the script's host so it survives restarts.
questions.explanation: Access tokens expire after 30 minutes. The refresh_token grant exchanges a refresh token for a new access_token + new refresh_token; both should be captured for the next cycle. Re-running client_credentials works but is wasteful and unnecessary.

questions.prompt: Your webhook endpoint receives a POST from YCM with an X-Signature header. How do you verify the request is genuine?
choices: Check that the request came from a Yeastar-owned IP address.
Compute base64(HMAC-SHA256(raw_body, your_webhook_secret)) and constant-time-compare against the X-Signature header value.
Decode the X-Signature header as a JWT and verify the signature with YCM's public key.
Trust the request if the User-Agent contains 'Yeastar'.
questions.explanation: The X-Signature is HMAC-SHA256 of the raw body keyed by the per-webhook secret YCM generated, base64-encoded. You compute the same value and compare; if they match, the request came from someone with the secret (i.e. YCM). IP whitelisting helps but is not the documented integrity check.
